justification: Cultural socialization in childhood is inherently multi-dimensional, spanning parental discussion habits, museum visits, book gifting, independent reading frequency, and household book presence — no single item captures the full construct. The questions use broadly compatible ordinal scales (4- and 6-point frequency/presence scales) and all load conceptually onto a latent 'culturally enriched home environment' factor, making PCA appropriate to extract the dominant shared variance. Binning the first principal component into 5 quantiles yields an ordinal variable with adequate spread while respecting the ≤15 category constraint.

The six items in this cluster span conceptually distinct sub-dimensions (cultural pluralism preferences, foreign influence perceptions, preservation priorities, and national affect), making aggregation into a single coherent score theoretically incoherent and scale-incompatible. p52 — the emotion respondent feels about Mexico — has the highest entropy (H=2.46) among all items and serves as a holistic affective summary of national identity orientation, which is the core of this construct. Its 9 categories are within the ≤15 limit and provide maximum discriminatory power for the bridge regression.

The construct captures a latent dimension of ethnocultural embeddedness and linguistic repertoire; the binary/ordinal capability and identity items (p34, p35, p37, p48, p49, p54) all contribute additive evidence of indigenous or minority cultural capital, making a sum score conceptually appropriate. The language-specific detail variables (p34_1a_1, p35_1a_1, p49_2a_1) are excluded because they are nominal identifiers of language/heritage type rather than ordinal indicators of degree, and their very low entropy (H≤0.33) reflects near-zero variance that would distort the composite. Summing the six ordinal capability and identity items and binning to ≤5 categories produces a parsimonious ordinal index of ethnocultural capital suitable for the bridge model.

The items in this cluster mix fundamentally incompatible scales — binary presence (p15a_1), 3-point frequency (p15c_2–4), 4-point presence (p14), 7-point ease (p16), and 11-category barrier (p17) — making a meaningful composite score unreliable without extensive rescaling. p18_1, asking about the perceived purpose or function of reading, has the highest entropy (H=2.46) and best captures the respondent's literacy orientation as a dispositional attitude rather than a behavioral frequency, which is the theoretically richer dimension for cross-survey bridging. Its 8 categories fall within the ≤15 limit and provide strong discriminatory signal.

Subjective SES is a well-established multi-component construct combining self-assessed economic situation, social class identification, life satisfaction, parental education, parental occupation, and school type — each tapping a distinct facet of socioeconomic position that no single item fully represents. The scales are heterogeneous in range (5–15 categories) and conceptual type (subjective perception vs. objective background), so PCA is preferred over a raw sum to extract the dominant latent SES gradient while down-weighting idiosyncratic item variance. Binning the first principal component into 5 quantiles produces a well-distributed ordinal SES variable that maximizes bridging utility across surveys sharing demographic anchors.
